meta.title: Security
meta.description: Cross-cutting security review checklist applicable to any stack

items.text: Are credentials hashed with a strong, salted algorithm (bcrypt, argon2)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Passwords must be hashed with a slow, salted algorithm designed for password storage. MD5, SHA-256, and unsalted hashes are not sufficient.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If the database is breached, weak hashes let attackers recover passwords in minutes. bcrypt/argon2 make brute force infeasible (seconds per guess instead of billions per second).
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for password storage code. Check which hashing algorithm is used. Verify a unique salt is generated per password (bcrypt and argon2 handle this automatically).
baseContent.exampleComment: Passwords are hashed with SHA-256. This is a fast hash, making brute force easy. Switch to bcrypt with a cost factor of at least 12.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
import hashlib
hashed = hashlib.sha256(password.encode()).hexdigest()
# Fast hash, no salt, easily cracked

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import bcrypt
hashed = bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode(), bcrypt.gensalt(rounds=12))
# Slow hash, auto-salted, brute-force resistant

baseContent.keyTakeaway: bcrypt or argon2id for passwords. Never MD5, SHA-1, or SHA-256.

items.text: Are sessions managed securely (expiry, rotation, invalidation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Sessions should expire after inactivity, session IDs should be rotated after login (to prevent fixation), and logout should invalidate the session server-side.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Long-lived sessions increase the window for hijacking. Session fixation lets attackers set a known session ID before login. Client-only logout leaves the session valid.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check session timeout configuration. Verify the session ID changes after authentication. Confirm logout destroys the server-side session, not just the cookie.
baseContent.exampleComment: The session ID is not rotated after login. An attacker who set the session cookie before login can hijack the authenticated session.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Rotate session IDs on login. Expire on inactivity. Invalidate server-side on logout.

items.text: Is multi-factor authentication supported for sensitive operations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Critical operations (password change, payment, admin actions) should require a second factor or re-authentication, even if the user is already logged in.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A stolen session token grants access to everything. Re-authentication for sensitive operations limits the blast radius of session hijacking.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if sensitive operations require re-entering password, TOTP, or other second factor. Is MFA available for user accounts?
baseContent.exampleComment: Password changes do not require the current password. An attacker with a stolen session could change the password and lock out the real user.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Re-authenticate for sensitive operations. Support MFA.

items.text: Are JWTs validated correctly (signature, expiry, issuer, audience)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: JWT validation must check: signature (with the correct algorithm, not 'none'), expiry (exp claim), issuer (iss), and audience (aud). Algorithm confusion attacks are common.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Skipping any validation step lets attackers forge tokens. The 'alg: none' attack bypasses signatures entirely. Missing expiry checks mean tokens are valid forever.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check JWT verification code. Is the algorithm explicitly specified (not read from the token header)? Are exp, iss, and aud validated?
baseContent.exampleComment: The JWT library reads the algorithm from the token header. An attacker can set alg=none to bypass signature verification. Specify the expected algorithm explicitly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Hardcode the expected algorithm. Validate exp, iss, and aud. Never trust the token's alg header.

items.text: Is authorization enforced server-side for every endpoint?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every API endpoint must check that the authenticated user has permission to perform the action. Client-side UI hiding is not authorization.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing server-side authorization is the most common high-severity vulnerability. Attackers bypass the UI and call APIs directly. OWASP ranks this as A01 (Broken Access Control).
baseContent.howToVerify: For each endpoint, check: is there an auth check? Does it verify both authentication (who) and authorization (can they)? Test by calling the endpoint as a different user.
baseContent.exampleComment: The /admin/users endpoint checks if the user is authenticated but not if they are an admin. Any logged-in user can list all users.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every endpoint checks authorization. UI hiding is not security.

items.text: Are object references validated against the current user (IDOR prevention)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When a user requests /orders/123, the server must verify that order 123 belongs to the requesting user. Otherwise any user can access any order by guessing IDs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) is trivial to exploit. Attackers increment IDs or use enumeration to access other users' data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check every endpoint that takes a resource ID. Does the query filter by the authenticated user? Test by requesting another user's resource.
baseContent.exampleComment: GET /api/invoices/{id} fetches by ID without checking ownership. Add a WHERE userId = currentUser filter to prevent IDOR.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always filter by the authenticated user's ID. Never trust client-supplied resource IDs alone.

items.text: Can users escalate their own privileges?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Users should not be able to change their own role, access level, or permissions. The API should ignore role fields in user-submitted data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mass assignment or unprotected role fields let users promote themselves to admin. This is a complete authorization bypass.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check user update endpoints. Can a user send { role: 'admin' } and have it accepted? Are role changes restricted to admin-only endpoints?
baseContent.exampleComment: The PUT /users/me endpoint accepts a 'role' field from the request body. A user could set themselves as admin. Strip the role field from user-submitted updates.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never accept role or permission changes from the user being modified.

items.text: Do service accounts and API keys follow least-privilege?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Service accounts, API keys, and database users should have the minimum permissions needed. A read-only service should not have write access.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Over-privileged credentials increase blast radius. A compromised read-only key is annoying. A compromised admin key is catastrophic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check IAM policies, database user permissions, and API key scopes. Can any be narrowed without breaking functionality?
baseContent.exampleComment: The application database user has SUPERUSER privileges. Restrict to SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on application tables only.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Grant minimum permissions. Separate read and write credentials where possible.

items.text: Are all SQL queries parameterized (no string concatenation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: SQL queries must use parameterized statements or an ORM's query builder. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SQL injection can read, modify, or delete any data in the database. It can also execute system commands in some configurations. It remains the most dangerous injection attack.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for raw SQL strings. Check for string concatenation or template literals containing user input. Verify ORM raw query methods use parameterized forms.
baseContent.exampleComment: This query concatenates the search term directly into SQL. Use a parameterized query with $1 placeholder.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always parameterize. Never concatenate user input into SQL.

items.text: Is user-generated content properly escaped to prevent XSS?

baseContent.whatItMeans: User input rendered in HTML must be escaped (or sanitized if HTML is intentional). Modern frameworks (React, Angular) auto-escape by default, but dangerouslySetInnerHTML and similar bypass this.
baseContent.whyItMatters: XSS lets attackers execute JavaScript in other users' browsers, stealing sessions, credentials, and personal data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for dangerouslySetInnerHTML, innerHTML, v-html, [innerHTML], and raw HTML rendering. Check that user input is escaped in server-rendered templates.
baseContent.exampleComment: dangerouslySetInnerHTML is used with user-provided content. Sanitize with DOMPurify or render as text instead.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
{userComment}
// Or if HTML is needed:

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Let the framework escape output. Sanitize if raw HTML is intentional.

items.text: Are OS commands avoided, or parameterized if necessary?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Executing shell commands with user input (exec, system, os.popen) enables command injection. Use language-native libraries instead, or parameterize with execFile/spawn.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Command injection gives attackers full system access. A single semicolon in user input can chain arbitrary commands.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for exec, system, popen, subprocess.shell=True, child_process.exec with user input. Replace with parameterized alternatives.
baseContent.exampleComment: child_process.exec with user input allows command injection. Use execFile with an argument array instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const { exec } = require('child_process');
exec(`convert ${userFilename} output.png`); // injection via filename

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid shell commands. If unavoidable, use parameterized execution (execFile, not exec).

items.text: Is file path traversal prevented when handling user-supplied paths?

baseContent.whatItMeans: User-supplied filenames or paths must be validated to prevent ../../../etc/passwd style attacks. Resolve the path and verify it stays within the allowed directory.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Path traversal can read any file on the server (credentials, source code, private keys). Combined with write access, it enables arbitrary code execution.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for user-supplied filenames used in file operations. Is the resolved path checked against the allowed base directory?
baseContent.exampleComment: The filename from the request is passed directly to fs.readFile. An attacker can use ../../../etc/passwd. Resolve the path and verify it starts with the upload directory.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Resolve the full path and verify it stays within the allowed directory.

items.text: Are server-side requests validated to prevent SSRF?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When the server fetches a URL provided by the user (webhooks, image proxies, link previews), it must validate the URL to prevent requests to internal services.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SSRF lets attackers probe internal networks, access metadata services (169.254.169.254), and reach services behind firewalls. It was the root cause of the Capital One breach.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for user-controlled URLs passed to fetch/http.get/requests. Is the URL validated? Are internal IPs blocked? Is DNS rebinding prevented?
baseContent.exampleComment: The webhook URL is fetched without validation. An attacker could set it to http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ to access AWS credentials.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate and allowlist URLs for server-side requests. Block internal IPs and metadata endpoints.

items.text: Are secrets, API keys, and credentials kept out of source code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Secrets must come from environment variables, secret managers (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager), or encrypted config. They should never appear in source code, even in tests.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Secrets in code end up in version control history forever. Even private repos get cloned to developer laptops, CI systems, and backups. One leak compromises all.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for strings matching API key patterns, connection strings with passwords, and private keys. Check .env files are in .gitignore.
baseContent.exampleComment: This API key is hardcoded in the source file. Move it to an environment variable and reference via process.env.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: No secrets in code. Use environment variables or a secrets manager.

items.text: Are .env files excluded from version control?

baseContent.whatItMeans: .env files containing secrets must be in .gitignore. Only .env.example (with placeholder values) should be committed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A committed .env file exposes all application secrets. Git history retains deleted files, so removing it later is not enough.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check .gitignore for .env patterns. Run git ls-files | grep env to see if any .env files are tracked.
baseContent.exampleComment: The .env file is committed with database credentials. Add .env to .gitignore, rotate the exposed credentials, and use git filter-branch to remove from history.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: .env in .gitignore. .env.example in the repo with placeholders. Rotate any exposed secrets.

items.text: Are secrets excluded from logs, error messages, and stack traces?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Log statements, error responses, and exception messages must not include tokens, passwords, or PII. Redact sensitive fields before logging.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Logs are stored in multiple systems (ELK, CloudWatch, Splunk), often with broad access. Secrets in logs spread to every system that ingests them.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for log.info, console.log, logger.error that might include request bodies, tokens, or passwords. Check error responses for stack traces in production.
baseContent.exampleComment: The authentication error log includes the password attempt. Redact it: log.warn('Auth failed for user', { userId, reason: 'invalid_password' }).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never log secrets or PII. Redact before logging.

items.text: Do secrets have a rotation plan and are they not shared across environments?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Production, staging, and development should use different credentials. Secrets should be rotatable without code changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Shared secrets between environments mean a dev leak compromises production. Non-rotatable secrets mean a breach requires code deployment to fix.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the same API keys are used across environments. Verify secrets can be rotated by changing environment variables, not code.
baseContent.exampleComment: The same Stripe API key is used in dev and production. Use test keys for dev and restrict production keys to the production environment.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Different secrets per environment. Rotation without code changes.

items.text: Is all data transmitted over TLS/HTTPS?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All HTTP traffic should be HTTPS. HTTP should redirect to HTTPS. HSTS headers should be set. Internal service-to-service calls should also use TLS.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unencrypted traffic can be intercepted on any network hop. Credentials, tokens, and PII are exposed to anyone on the network path.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for http:// URLs in configuration (should be https://). Verify HSTS headers. Check that HTTP requests redirect to HTTPS.
baseContent.exampleComment: The API base URL uses http://. Change to https:// and add Strict-Transport-Security header.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: HTTPS everywhere. HSTS headers. No exceptions for internal traffic.

items.text: Is sensitive data encrypted at rest?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PII, financial data, and health records should be encrypted in the database. Use application-level encryption for the most sensitive fields, and disk-level encryption as a baseline.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Database backups, stolen disks, and unauthorized database access expose unencrypted data. Encryption at rest is often a compliance requirement (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check what data is stored. Is PII (SSN, payment info) encrypted? Is disk encryption enabled on the database? Are encryption keys managed separately from data?
baseContent.exampleComment: Credit card numbers are stored in plain text. Use application-level encryption with a key stored in a secrets manager.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Encrypt PII and financial data at rest. Keys in a secrets manager, not the database.

items.text: Is PII collection minimized and necessary data justified?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Only collect data you actually need. Do not store birthdates if you only need to verify age. Do not log IP addresses if you do not need them for security.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Every piece of PII stored is a liability. More data means more risk in a breach and more compliance burden (GDPR right to be forgotten, data subject requests).
baseContent.howToVerify: Review new fields being added. Is each one necessary? Can the same goal be achieved with less data (e.g., age range instead of birthdate)?
baseContent.exampleComment: The signup form collects phone number and address, but neither is used by the app. Remove these fields to reduce PII exposure.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Collect only what you need. Every field is a liability.

items.text: Is CORS configured to allow only trusted origins?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing headers must specify which domains can access the API. Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials is a security vulnerability.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Overly permissive CORS allows any website to make authenticated requests to your API on behalf of the user, enabling data theft.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CORS configuration. Is the origin allowlist explicit? Is Access-Control-Allow-Credentials used with a wildcard origin (this is blocked by browsers but signals misconfiguration)?
baseContent.exampleComment: CORS allows all origins (*) with credentials. Restrict to your app's domain(s) only.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Explicit origin allowlist. Never wildcard with credentials.

items.text: Is all input validated server-side, not just client-side?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Client-side validation is for UX. Server-side validation is for security. Attackers bypass the client entirely. Every input must be validated where it is consumed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Client-side validation can be bypassed with curl, Postman, or browser dev tools. Server-side validation is the only reliable boundary.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check API endpoints. Is the request body validated (types, lengths, ranges, formats)? Are query parameters validated? Is validation duplicated on the server?
baseContent.exampleComment: The email is validated only in the React form. The API accepts any string. Add server-side email validation.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate on the server. Client validation is optional UX polish.

items.text: Is the Content-Type header validated for API requests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API endpoints should validate that the Content-Type header matches what they expect (application/json). Accepting unexpected types can enable CSRF or parsing confusion.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Browsers can send cross-origin POST requests with text/plain Content-Type, which bypasses CSRF protections that rely on rejecting non-JSON requests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the API rejects requests with incorrect Content-Type. Test with Content-Type: text/plain on JSON endpoints.
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint accepts any Content-Type. Reject requests that are not application/json to prevent CSRF via form submission.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate Content-Type. Reject unexpected types.

items.text: Are file uploads validated for type, size, and content?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Validate file extensions, MIME types, and actual file content (magic bytes). Enforce size limits. Store uploaded files outside the web root with random names.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unvalidated file uploads enable remote code execution (uploading a .php or .jsp file), XSS (SVG with JavaScript), and denial of service (huge files).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check upload endpoints. Is the file type validated by content, not just extension? Is there a size limit? Are files stored outside the web root?
baseContent.exampleComment: This upload validates only the file extension. An attacker can upload a .jpg file containing PHP code. Validate the file's magic bytes.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate file content, not just extension. Size limits. Store outside web root.

items.text: Is rate limiting applied to authentication and sensitive endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Login, registration, password reset, and other sensitive endpoints should have rate limits to prevent brute force attacks and abuse.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without rate limiting, attackers can try millions of password combinations, enumerate valid emails, or flood the system with registrations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if login endpoints have rate limiting. Is it per-IP, per-user, or both? Are other sensitive endpoints (password reset, email verification) rate-limited?
baseContent.exampleComment: The login endpoint has no rate limiting. Add a limit of 5 attempts per minute per IP, with exponential backoff.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Rate limit login and sensitive endpoints. Per-IP and per-account.

items.text: Are dependencies free of known vulnerabilities?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Run npm audit, pip audit, cargo audit, or equivalent to check for known CVEs in dependencies. Address critical and high vulnerabilities before merging.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Known vulnerabilities in popular libraries are actively exploited. Automated scanners find and attack vulnerable versions within hours of disclosure.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run the language's audit tool. Check CI for automated vulnerability scanning (Dependabot, Snyk, Socket). Review new dependencies for advisories.
baseContent.exampleComment: npm audit shows 2 critical vulnerabilities in transitive dependencies. Update the parent package or use overrides to fix.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run audit tools. Fix critical/high vulnerabilities before merge. Automate in CI.

items.text: Are new dependencies from trusted sources with active maintenance?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Before adding a dependency, check: author reputation, download count, maintenance status, license, and transitive dependency count. Prefer widely-used, well-maintained packages.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Supply chain attacks (event-stream, ua-parser-js, colors) inject malicious code via popular packages. Each dependency is code you implicitly trust.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check npm/PyPI/crates.io for the package. When was it last updated? How many maintainers? What is the download count? How many transitive dependencies?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new dependency has 12 weekly downloads and no updates in 3 years. Consider a more established alternative or implement the functionality directly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Vet new dependencies. Check maintenance, popularity, and transitive depth.

items.text: Is the lockfile committed and reviewed for unexpected changes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Lockfiles (package-lock.json, yarn.lock, Gemfile.lock) pin exact dependency versions. They should be committed and any changes reviewed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a committed lockfile, different environments install different versions, which can introduce vulnerabilities or break builds. Malicious lockfile changes can downgrade to vulnerable versions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that the lockfile is committed. Review lockfile changes in the PR for unexpected additions, removals, or version changes.
baseContent.exampleComment: The lockfile shows 15 new transitive dependencies from adding one package. Review these additions for known issues.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Commit lockfiles. Review changes. Unexpected updates are a red flag.

items.text: Are stack traces and internal errors hidden from API responses?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Production error responses should return generic messages. Stack traces, file paths, SQL queries, and internal service names help attackers understand the system.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stack traces reveal framework versions, file structure, database schema, and third-party services. This information accelerates targeted attacks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Trigger an error in the API (invalid input, server error). Does the response include stack traces, SQL queries, or internal paths?
baseContent.exampleComment: The 500 response includes a full stack trace with file paths and database query. Return a generic error message and log the details server-side.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Generic error messages to clients. Full details in server logs only.

items.text: Are security-relevant events logged (login failures, permission denials)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Log authentication successes and failures, authorization denials, input validation failures, and admin actions. Include enough context to investigate incidents.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without security logs, you cannot detect brute force attacks, unauthorized access attempts, or compromised accounts. Logs are essential for incident response.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if login failures, permission denials, and admin actions generate log entries. Do logs include user ID, IP, timestamp, and action?
baseContent.exampleComment: Failed login attempts are not logged. Add logging with user identifier and IP address (not the password) for security monitoring.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Log security events: auth failures, permission denials, admin actions. Never log secrets.

items.text: Are constant-time comparisons used for secrets and tokens?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Comparing secrets with === leaks information through timing differences. Use constant-time comparison functions (crypto.timingSafeEqual, hmac.compare_digest).
baseContent.whyItMatters: String comparison short-circuits on the first different byte. By measuring response time, attackers can determine the correct prefix byte-by-byte.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for direct string comparison of API keys, HMAC signatures, or tokens. These should use constant-time comparison.
baseContent.exampleComment: The webhook signature is compared with ===. Use crypto.timingSafeEqual to prevent timing attacks.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use constant-time comparison for all secret comparisons.

items.text: Are security headers configured (CSP, X-Frame-Options, etc.)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options (or frame-ancestors CSP), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy headers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Security headers are defense-in-depth. CSP mitigates XSS even if escaping fails. X-Frame-Options prevents clickjacking. These are low-effort, high-impact protections.
baseContent.howToVerify: Use securityheaders.com or browser dev tools to check response headers. Verify CSP is not overly permissive (unsafe-inline, unsafe-eval).
baseContent.exampleComment: No Content-Security-Policy header is set. Add a restrictive CSP that allows only your domains and avoids unsafe-inline.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, and Referrer-Policy on all responses.

items.text: Is CSRF protection in place for state-changing requests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: State-changing requests (POST, PUT, DELETE) with cookie-based auth need CSRF protection: anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies, or requiring a custom header.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without CSRF protection, a malicious website can trick the user's browser into making authenticated requests (transferring money, changing email) without the user's knowledge.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the app uses cookie-based auth. If so, is there a CSRF token or SameSite=Strict cookie? Token-based auth (Bearer header) is inherently CSRF-safe.
baseContent.exampleComment: This form uses cookie-based auth without a CSRF token. Add a CSRF token or set SameSite=Strict on the session cookie.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Cookie auth needs CSRF protection. Bearer tokens are inherently safe. SameSite=Strict helps.

items.text: Are cookies set with Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite flags?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Secure: only sent over HTTPS. HttpOnly: inaccessible to JavaScript. SameSite: controls cross-origin sending. All session cookies should have all three.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without Secure, cookies can be stolen on HTTP connections. Without HttpOnly, XSS can read them. Without SameSite, they are sent on cross-origin requests (CSRF).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check Set-Cookie headers. Do session cookies have Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite=Lax (or Strict)?
baseContent.exampleComment: The session cookie is missing HttpOnly. JavaScript can read it, making XSS attacks more damaging. Add HttpOnly to the cookie options.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Session cookies: Secure + HttpOnly + SameSite=Lax. Always.
